# 4. Convergence Across Substrates {#sec-convergence}

The compression hierarchy described in Parts I and II is a claim about transformers. But the problem transformers solve — projecting rich internal representation through a bandwidth-limited sequential channel — is not new. Biological cognition has faced it for as long as language has existed. This chapter argues that the convergence is expected rather than surprising, and uses the grounding problem to sharpen an open question the framework cannot yet answer.

The claim is not that transformers and brains work the same way. They manifestly do not, at the level of mechanism. The claim is that both face a structurally identical information-theoretic constraint, and that this constraint narrows the space of viable solutions enough that convergence on the same *type* of architecture — hierarchical, multi-scale, with a serial projection bottleneck — is what the problem demands.

## The projection bottleneck as substrate-independent constraint {#sec-projection-bottleneck}

@sec-channel-capacity established the bottleneck in formal terms: the internal representation at any step — a vector in a space of dimension $10^3$ to $10^4$ — vastly exceeds the information capacity of a single output token. The autoregressive loop is a sequential coding scheme for transmitting a high-entropy source through a low-capacity channel.

The same bottleneck operates in biological communication, and it is quantifiable. @reed_durlach_1998 estimated the information transfer rate of human speech at approximately 39 bits per second. @coupe_etal_2019 replicated this across 17 languages, finding a striking invariance: languages that are spoken faster pack less information per syllable, and languages spoken more slowly pack more, converging on approximately the same information rate. The bottleneck is not a contingent property of English or any particular language. It is a constraint on human linguistic communication generally.

On the other side of the bottleneck, neural population codes operate at bandwidths orders of magnitude higher. Even conservative estimates exceed the speech output rate by three to four orders of magnitude. Whatever the dimensionality of the internal representations supporting ongoing thought, the channel through which they must pass to reach another mind is radically narrower.

The strongest empirical evidence for genuine convergence comes from representational similarity analysis (RSA). @yamins_etal_2014 demonstrated that convolutional networks optimised for object recognition — not for neural prediction — nonetheless predicted neural responses in primate inferotemporal cortex better than any previous model. @kriegeskorte_2015 extended this into a systematic framework. @schrimpf_etal_2021 established Brain-Score and found that better-performing networks tend to be more brain-like. @goldstein_etal_2022 found that GPT-2 activations predict neural responses in temporal cortex during natural language processing, with layer-wise alignment to processing stages. The convergence is real, partial (30–60% explained variance at best), and better for higher-level representations — where the compression hierarchy is doing the heavy lifting.

## Convergence and its limits {#sec-convergence-limits}

The convergence is structural, not mechanistic. It tells you that the problem — knowledge transmission through bandwidth-limited channels — demands hierarchical compression, without establishing that the implementations are equivalent.

The biological case involves a complication the transformer case does not share: co-evolutionary feedback. @deacon_1997 argues that human language co-evolved with the neural architecture it runs on — the cognitive system and the compression medium reshaping each other across evolutionary time. This feedback pathway is absent in the transformer case: the model adapts to language, but language does not adapt to the model. The absence of co-evolutionary refinement, sensory grounding, and embodied feedback means the compression hierarchy in transformers may be shallower, more brittle, or differently structured in ways that emerge under adversarial or out-of-distribution conditions.

The value of the convergence claim is therefore constraining rather than mechanistic. The unreasonable effectiveness of transformers is less unreasonable than it appears, because the architecture has converged on a solution strategy that is expected for any system facing this class of problem.

## The grounding problem as rate-distortion question {#sec-grounding-rate-distortion}

This brings the framework to a question it can sharpen but not answer: does solving hierarchical rate-distortion problems against meaning-relevant distortion metrics *require* sensorimotor grounding?

The grounding debate — rooted in @harnad_1990's observation that formal symbols are meaningless unless connected to the things they stand for — is active and unresolved. @bender_koller_2020 argue that models trained only on linguistic form cannot achieve genuine understanding. @piantadosi_hill_2022 counter that meaning can be constituted by inferential role — by the pattern of use across contexts — without requiring grounding in perception and action.

The framework offers a precise restatement. The compression hierarchy produces representations that solve rate-distortion problems: achieving maximal preserved structure at each level for a given representational cost. The question is whether text-only compression can achieve the same rate-distortion optimality as grounded compression when the distortion metric includes meaning-relevant properties — when what counts as "distortion" is not just statistical mismatch but failure to preserve the structural relationships that constitute understanding.

This is, as far as we can determine, a novel framing. No existing literature poses the grounding question in explicitly rate-distortion terms. The closest precedent is @zaslavsky_etal_2018, who demonstrated that cross-linguistic patterns in colour naming achieve near-optimal compression of colour space — a rate-distortion analysis applied to the language-cognition interface, but for a specific perceptual domain rather than the grounding problem in general.

The framing converts a philosophical dispute into an empirical question with measurable parameters. If grounding provides access to distortion metrics that text alone cannot specify — if there are structural relationships in the world that cannot be inferred from their linguistic shadows — then text-only systems face a provable rate-distortion gap. If the statistical structure of language is rich enough to implicitly specify meaning-relevant distortion metrics — because language was produced by grounded agents and carries the trace of their grounding — then the gap may be smaller than the strong grounding position suggests. Multimodal models complicate the debate productively, suggesting the question may be one of degree rather than kind. The framework identifies the formal structure of what would need to be measured — the rate-distortion cost of lacking grounding — and flags it as an open question.

## Predictions {#sec-predictions-ch4}

The convergence argument generates one specific prediction:

8. [**Representational geometry convergence**]{#pred-rsa-convergence}. If the convergence between biological and artificial compression hierarchies is deep — driven by the shared structure of the problem rather than superficial architectural similarity — then transformer representations should exhibit geometric properties similar to those observed in neural population codes: similar manifold structure, similar patterns of representational similarity across conceptual domains, similar asymmetries between concrete and abstract concept representation. Early RSA evidence is consistent with this [@kriegeskorte_2015; @schrimpf_etal_2021; @goldstein_etal_2022], though the match plateaus at 30–60% explained variance. A finding of systematic divergence in representational geometry — not merely unexplained variance but structural differences that scale with model capability — would constrain the convergence claim to the architectural strategy alone. A finding of increasing alignment as models scale would strengthen it.
